questions.question: Why do compilers use an intermediate representation instead of going directly from AST to machine code?
options: IR is smaller than the AST so it saves memory
IR enables machine-independent optimization and supports multiple backends
IR is required by the C standard for portability
ASTs cannot represent control flow
questions.explanation: IR decouples the front-end (parsing, type checking) from the back-end (code generation). This allows optimizations that are independent of the target machine and lets one front-end target many architectures.

questions.question: In three-address code, what does 'three-address' refer to?
options: Each instruction references three memory locations
Each instruction has at most two operands and one result
Each instruction can jump to three different labels
Instructions are stored in a three-dimensional array
questions.explanation: Three-address instructions have the form `dest = src1 op src2` — at most two source operands and one destination. This maps naturally to register-based hardware.

questions.question: When lowering `a + b * c` to three-address code, what temporary names would be generated?
options: t1 = a + b; t2 = t1 * c
t1 = b * c; t2 = a + t1
t1 = a + b * c
questions.explanation: The generator recursively processes subexpressions. It first flattens `b * c` into `t1 = b * c`, then uses `t1` as an operand in `t2 = a + t1`. Each temporary holds exactly one intermediate result.

questions.question: What is a basic block?
options: A single IR instruction
A maximal sequence of instructions with one entry point and one exit point
Any sequence of instructions between two labels
A function body without loops
questions.explanation: A basic block is a straight-line sequence where control enters only at the first instruction and leaves only at the last. It is the fundamental unit for data-flow analysis and optimization.

questions.question: Why do compilers use an intermediate representation (IR) instead of generating target code directly from the AST?
options: Because the AST is too small to hold all information
Because IR enables target-independent optimizations and retargeting to different architectures
Because direct code generation is impossible
Because ASTs are only used during parsing
questions.explanation: An IR decouples the frontend (parsing, type checking) from the backend (code generation). Optimizations like constant folding, dead code elimination, and loop unrolling work on the IR without knowing the target. To support a new CPU, only the backend needs to change.
